EMS2026-1-1-1-2-2-01.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:8.
EMS2026-1-1-1-2-2-01.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Discipline the three crews because missing reassessment after opioid use is a patient-safety issue.
options.B: Remove the measure because documentation compliance is not a clinical outcome.
options.C: Analyze workflow or electronic-record barriers nonpunitively, feed results back, and reserve discipline for reckless or willful behavior.
options.D: Require a one-time lecture and close the issue because recurring chart review can feel punitive.

EMS2026-1-1-1-2-2-01.rationale: A widespread process defect should trigger nonpunitive process analysis and feedback, while discipline is reserved for reckless or willful behavior. A treats a system signal as only individual failure, B ignores a safety process, and D lacks measurement of improvement.
EMS2026-1-1-1-2-2-01.stem: A monthly review finds that pain reassessment after fentanyl is missing in 42% of charts across all shifts. Supervisors want to discipline the last three crews with missing reassessments. Which first response best fits quality improvement?
EMS2026-1-1-1-2-2-01.title: QI Versus Discipline For A Process Defect

EMS2026-1-1-2-1-02.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:148.
EMS2026-1-1-2-1-02.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: EMS creates individualized duty for community members once dispatch classifies a call as potentially life threatening.
options.B: Public duty becomes special duty at computer-aided dispatch entry because the system has accepted a request and assigned priority.
options.C: Public duty to the community differs from special duty to a specific patient after the system undertakes care.
options.D: Special duty generally begins at hospital arrival because prehospital clinicians operate under system-level public duty.

EMS2026-1-1-2-1-02.rationale: Public duty to a community is different from a special duty to a specific individual patient. A and B set individualized duty too early, while D sets it too late and mischaracterizes EMS clinical care.
EMS2026-1-1-2-1-02.stem: During a severe storm, dispatch delays occur across a county. One call involves a patient not yet assigned a unit; another involves a patient already being treated by an EMS crew. A trainee asks why the legal duty analysis differs. Which explanation is best?
EMS2026-1-1-2-1-02.title: Public Duty Versus Special Duty

EMS2026-1-1-2-1-06.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:152.
EMS2026-1-1-2-1-06.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: EMS crews determine legal competence whenever a refusal is signed, risks are documented, and the patient appears oriented.
options.B: EMS clinicians assess decision-making capacity for the encounter; legal competence is a broader legal determination.
options.C: High-risk complaints cannot be refused until a physician evaluates the patient in person and repeats the risk discussion.
options.D: Capacity assessment is unnecessary if the refusal form lists the risks of refusing transport and a witness signs.

EMS2026-1-1-2-1-06.rationale: EMS clinicians assess decision-making capacity for a specific decision; legal competence is a broader legal determination. A and C misstate the EMS role, and D treats documentation as a substitute for assessment.
EMS2026-1-1-2-1-06.stem: A 58-year-old with chest pain refuses transport. The patient repeats the myocardial infarction concern, describes risk of death, explains the reason for refusing, and states a plan to call family and seek care if symptoms worsen. The crew asks whether they are certifying legal competence. Which teaching is best?
EMS2026-1-1-2-1-06.title: Capacity Versus Competence

EMS2026-1-1-2-4-2-03.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:520.
EMS2026-1-1-2-4-2-03.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: He is legally incompetent, so EMS must obtain a court order before any treatment or transport.
options.B: He lacks decision-making capacity for this refusal because he cannot appreciate risk or reason about the decision, even though competence is a legal determination.
options.C: He has capacity because he can repeat the diagnosis EMS gave him and clearly says he does not want transport.
options.D: He has capacity unless he is unconscious, intoxicated, or under an existing guardianship order.

EMS2026-1-1-2-4-2-03.rationale: Capacity is decision-specific and includes understanding, appreciation, reasoning, and communication. A uses the wrong legal construct for field assessment, C overweights repetition, and D omits delirium or psychosis impairing appreciation and reasoning.
EMS2026-1-1-2-4-2-03.stem: A patient with pneumonia refuses transport. He can repeat that EMS thinks he has pneumonia, but he says oxygen is poisonous, cannot explain what could happen if he stays home, and believes the ambulance crew was sent by a television host. Which statement is most accurate?
EMS2026-1-1-2-4-2-03.title: Capacity Defect In A Refusal

EMS2026-1-1-2-7-06.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:178.
EMS2026-1-1-2-7-06.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Discipline the involved drivers, buy more insurance, and wait for claims frequency to change.
options.B: Identify the exposure, evaluate and rank the risk, implement controls such as policy or engineering changes, and revise based on results.
options.C: Investigate only crashes with injuries because minor property damage rarely predicts meaningful risk.
options.D: Write a new driving policy first and defer measurement until after the next annual safety review.

EMS2026-1-1-2-7-06.rationale: Risk management identifies exposures, evaluates potential, ranks risk, implements controls, and revises actions. A overfocuses on punishment and transfer, C misses warning events, and D implements without a complete risk process.
EMS2026-1-1-2-7-06.stem: An agency has three backing crashes in two months. One proposal is to discipline involved drivers; another is to review station design, spotter use, lighting, training, vehicle cameras, and policy. Which sequence best matches risk management?
EMS2026-1-1-2-7-06.title: Risk Management Applied To Crashes

EMS2026-1-2-4-2-01.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:21.
EMS2026-1-2-4-2-01.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: First responder, emergency medical technician, intermediate, paramedic, and critical care paramedic.
options.B: Emergency medical responder, emergency medical technician, intermediate, specialty care paramedic, and paramedic.
options.C: Emergency medical responder, emergency medical technician, advanced emergency medical technician, and paramedic.
options.D: Emergency care assistant, emergency medical technician, nurse-paramedic, physician assistant, and paramedic.

EMS2026-1-2-4-2-01.rationale: The National EMS Scope of Practice Model organizes entry-level EMS clinicians as emergency medical responder, emergency medical technician, advanced emergency medical technician, and paramedic. A, B, and D mix obsolete, local, or specialty categories with national entry-level categories.
EMS2026-1-2-4-2-01.stem: An agency policy still lists "basic," "intermediate," "specialty care paramedic," and "critical care paramedic" as national entry-level clinician categories. Which replacement best matches the National EMS Scope of Practice Model?
EMS2026-1-2-4-2-01.title: National EMS Clinician Levels
EMS2026-1-2-4-2-01.topic: EMS clinician levels (EMR, EMT, AEMT, Paramedic)

EMS2026-1-2-5-6-03.citation: Shelton SL, Swor RA, Domeier RM, Lucas R. Medical direction of interfacility transports. Prehosp Emerg Care. 2000;4(4):361.
EMS2026-1-2-5-6-03.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: The ambulance dispatcher, because the dispatcher sees available units and must balance regional coverage.
options.B: The transferring physician and facility select appropriate mode, personnel, and equipment.
options.C: The receiving physician, because the receiving hospital ultimately accepts the patient and destination risk.
options.D: The transport clinician, because the final decision occurs when the patient is moved to the stretcher.

EMS2026-1-2-5-6-03.rationale: The transferring physician and sending facility are responsible for arranging appropriate interfacility transport, including mode, personnel level, and equipment. A, C, and D may contribute to safety checks, but they do not replace the transferring physician's responsibility.
EMS2026-1-2-5-6-03.stem: A community physician orders a basic life support transfer for a ventilated patient on sedation because "the ambulance company can upgrade if needed." The transport agency asks who is responsible for selecting transport mode, personnel, and equipment. Which answer is best?
EMS2026-1-2-5-6-03.title: Interfacility Transport Responsibility

EMS2026-1-2-5-8-06.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:53.
EMS2026-1-2-5-8-06.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Secure a threatened airway before departure while also planning for volume loss, carbon monoxide exposure, temperature control, and transport-environment effects.
options.B: Avoid intubation before transport because speaking patients with burn injury usually maintain airway patency until arrival at a burn center.
options.C: Focus on wound dressing and analgesia first because airway edema is usually delayed until after the first hospital day.
options.D: Transport with high-flow oxygen only because airway intervention is safest after burn-surgeon evaluation.

EMS2026-1-2-5-8-06.rationale: Specialty burn transport should anticipate airway edema, carbon monoxide exposure, volume loss, temperature control, and transport effects before departure. B, C, and D understate the risk of losing a threatened airway en route.
EMS2026-1-2-5-8-06.stem: A patient with 35% total-body-surface-area burns is leaving a critical-access hospital for a burn center 90 minutes away. He has soot in the mouth, hoarseness, facial burns, and possible carbon monoxide exposure. The sending team asks whether to wait on intubation because he is still speaking. What is the best transport recommendation?
EMS2026-1-2-5-8-06.title: Burn Transport Airway Before Departure
EMS2026-1-2-5-8-06.topic: Critical care and specialty care transport

EMS2026-1-2-7-02.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:33.
EMS2026-1-2-7-02.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Add dispatch call-taking metrics, ambulance response intervals, and handoff times, then stop the map at emergency department arrival.
options.B: Add air medical dispatch, specialty ambulance availability, and interfacility transfer criteria because transport-mode selection is the main difference from EMS alone.
options.C: Add rehabilitation outcomes only when hospitals request EMS feedback after discharge or identify a prehospital process concern.
options.D: Include incident recognition, EMS response, specialty hospital care, prevention, rehabilitation, and system feedback across the full pathway.

EMS2026-1-2-7-02.rationale: An emergency medical care system is broader than EMS transport; it connects recognition, response, definitive care, prevention, rehabilitation, and evaluation. A and B remain transport-centric, while C treats rehabilitation as optional rather than part of the continuum.
EMS2026-1-2-7-02.stem: A regionalization committee is mapping care for major trauma. The draft begins with ambulance dispatch and ends at emergency department arrival. Which revision best reflects an emergency medical care system rather than EMS alone?
EMS2026-1-2-7-02.title: Emergency Medical Care System Scope

EMS2026-1-2-7-11.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:39.
EMS2026-1-2-7-11.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Define activation criteria, duration limits, time-critical exceptions, communication pathway, and regional override authority.
options.B: Define hospital-specific internal criteria and scheduled public-safety updates, but omit regional override authority.
options.C: Define time-critical exceptions and radio consultation, but leave diversion duration and regional saturation undefined.
options.D: Define duration limits and dashboard reporting, but let each hospital decide whether it can refuse specialty-center patients.

EMS2026-1-2-7-11.rationale: Diversion affects regional patient distribution and time-critical care, so it needs defined triggers, limits, exceptions, communication, and override authority. B lacks regional authority, C omits duration and saturation rules, and D leaves specialty-center access inconsistent.
EMS2026-1-2-7-11.stem: A regional diversion draft allows any hospital to declare diversion for crowding and requires ambulances to bypass that hospital. It does not define duration limits, trauma, ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), stroke exceptions, regional saturation rules, or who can override diversion. Which revision is most important before implementation?
EMS2026-1-2-7-11.title: Diversion Rule With Time-Critical Exceptions

EMS2026-1-3-1-1-01.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:240.
EMS2026-1-3-1-1-01.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Yes; the national model independently authorizes entry-level clinicians once it is published and adopted by education programs.
options.B: No; actual scope of practice is defined and limited by state law, even when the national model informs it.
options.C: Yes; a local medical director protocol can authorize the skill when the national model lists it, training exists, and quality review is planned.
options.D: No; national scope models apply only to paramedic practice and not to other EMS clinician levels.

EMS2026-1-3-1-1-01.rationale: The national model informs scope, but enforceable scope of practice is defined and limited by state statute or law. A and C overstate national-model or local-protocol authority, and D incorrectly narrows the model.
EMS2026-1-3-1-1-01.stem: A state EMS office is considering whether to authorize advanced emergency medical technicians to perform a procedure that appears in the National EMS Scope of Practice Model. A medical director asks whether inclusion in the national model automatically authorizes the procedure locally. Which answer is best?
EMS2026-1-3-1-1-01.title: Scope of Practice Authority

EMS2026-1-3-1-1-07.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:241.
EMS2026-1-3-1-1-07.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: It federally licenses EMS personnel for disaster work in all states after a national emergency declaration.
options.B: Member states can recognize EMS licenses from other compact states after enabling legislation.
options.C: It replaces state EMS offices with a national credentialing board during interstate mutual aid.
options.D: It applies only to physicians and nurses because EMS personnel are licensed solely by their home state.

EMS2026-1-3-1-1-07.rationale: The EMS Compact requires enabling state legislation and allows member states to recognize EMS personnel licensed in other member states for authorized duties. A makes it universal federal licensure, C replaces state authority, and D excludes EMS personnel contrary to the compact's purpose.
EMS2026-1-3-1-1-07.stem: During a hurricane deployment, an EMS agency wants to bring clinicians licensed in neighboring states. The receiving state participates in the EMS Compact. Which statement best describes the compact's relevance?
EMS2026-1-3-1-1-07.title: EMS Compact Authority

EMS2026-1-3-2-2-01.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:126.
EMS2026-1-3-2-2-01.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Use asynchronous cognitive prework, then perform local face-to-face skills practice and observed competency verification during each faculty visit.
options.B: Use synchronous video case discussion, require a passing quiz, and let agency educators sign off psychomotor competence during the next quarterly review.
options.C: Send airway videos and a checklist to each agency, then review first-month airway charts for correct device selection and ventilation outcomes.
options.D: Hold one regional workshop for crews who can attend, record the insertion demonstration, and accept attestation from crews who watch the recording later.

EMS2026-1-3-2-2-01.rationale: A hybrid model fits because the cognitive content can be delivered at distance while the psychomotor component still needs observed practice and verification. B and D defer or dilute the hands-on verification, and C substitutes chart outcomes for initial skills competence.
EMS2026-1-3-2-2-01.stem: A fellowship asks three rural EMS agencies to train paramedics on a new supraglottic airway. The cognitive material is unchanged across agencies, but learners must demonstrate sizing, insertion, troubleshooting, and failed-attempt recognition. Travel money covers only one faculty visit to each agency. Which design best matches the educational problem?
EMS2026-1-3-2-2-01.title: Hybrid Education For A Psychomotor Skill

EMS2026-1-3-2-6-06.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:135.
EMS2026-1-3-2-6-06.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Field training officer ride time for both clinicians, because direct observation is required before any remediation plan can be closed.
options.B: Agency-wide pediatric lecture for both clinicians, because common remediation preserves fairness and avoids singling out a provider.
options.C: Medication-calculation simulation with supervised field review for the dosing deficit; targeted documentation feedback and chart follow-up for the reassessment-record defect.
options.D: Discipline for the dosing deficit and no action for the documentation defect, because only the dosing error represents clinical risk.

EMS2026-1-3-2-6-06.rationale: Remediation should match the deficiency. C separates a competence problem from a documentation/process problem. A and B use one generic remedy for different defects, and D ignores the clinical and medicolegal risk of missing reassessment documentation.
EMS2026-1-3-2-6-06.stem: Chart review identifies two different problems after pediatric calls. One paramedic repeatedly miscalculates weight-based epinephrine doses; another has correct care but incomplete documentation of reassessment. Which remediation pairing is most defensible?
EMS2026-1-3-2-6-06.title: Remediation Matched To Defect Type

EMS2026-1-3-4-1-1-02.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:251.
EMS2026-1-3-4-1-1-02.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Ask the hospital to test the source patient first and arrange clinician evaluation only if the source test returns positive.
options.B: Clean the wound and finish the shift, then complete exposure documentation before going off duty if no symptoms develop.
options.C: Clean the wound, document the exposure, arrange prompt clinician evaluation, and initiate source and clinician testing through the designated process.
options.D: Start a workers-compensation report first because coverage determines which employee-health process is available.

EMS2026-1-3-4-1-1-02.rationale: Occupational exposure response starts with wound care, documentation, prompt clinician evaluation, and source and clinician testing through the designated process. B and D delay medical evaluation, and A incorrectly waits for source testing before evaluating the exposed clinician.
EMS2026-1-3-4-1-1-02.stem: A paramedic sustains a hollow-bore needle stick while transferring care. The source patient's infection status is unknown, and the crew is due for another call. Which immediate sequence is most appropriate?
EMS2026-1-3-4-1-1-02.title: Needlestick Exposure First Actions
EMS2026-1-3-4-1-1-02.topic: Regulations and standards (e.g., National Fire Protection Association [NFPA] 1582, Ryan White Act, Occupational Safety and Health Administration [OSHA] requirements, National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health [NIOSH])

EMS2026-1-3-4-3-03.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:105.
EMS2026-1-3-4-3-03.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: The score is normal because excessive daytime sleepiness begins at 15 and protocol recall is unrelated to fatigue.
options.B: A score of 10 or higher suggests excessive daytime sleepiness and fatigue-risk evaluation.
options.C: Treat the score as a high-risk sleep-disorder screen requiring occupational review and temporary driving restriction.
options.D: The score measures burnout rather than sleepiness and should be addressed through peer support rather than schedule review.

EMS2026-1-3-4-3-03.rationale: An ESS score of 10 or higher indicates excessive daytime sleepiness, which has been linked in EMS to protocol recall and vehicle-operation difficulty. A uses the wrong threshold, C treats a screening score as a diagnosis, and D confuses sleepiness measurement with burnout screening.
EMS2026-1-3-4-3-03.stem: After two minor ambulance backing crashes, an agency screens a night-shift crew member using the Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS). The clinician scores 13 and reports trouble remembering protocols near the end of shifts. Which interpretation best fits the result?
EMS2026-1-3-4-3-03.title: Sleepiness Scale Interpretation

EMS2026-1-3-4-4-04.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:118.
EMS2026-1-3-4-4-04.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Offer a brief out-of-service time-out with chosen peer support and reassess before return.
options.B: Require a formal group critical incident debriefing before either clinician leaves the station.
options.C: Send both clinicians home for the remainder of the week because early return to duty is harmful after pediatric calls.
options.D: Continue the shift and provide a wellness handout at the end because early discussion can reinforce distress.

EMS2026-1-3-4-4-04.rationale: A brief out-of-service time-out with chosen peer or supervisor support can be an appropriate early intervention after a significant stress event. B mandates a specific group process, C may overremove clinicians from work, and D provides too little immediate support.
EMS2026-1-3-4-4-04.stem: After a pediatric resuscitation, two clinicians are visibly distressed but want to finish the shift. There are enough units for coverage, and both identify trusted peers they would like to speak with. Which early intervention is most appropriate?
EMS2026-1-3-4-4-04.title: Immediate Stress Intervention
EMS2026-1-3-4-4-04.topic: Prevention and intervention for psychologically stressful events

EMS2026-1-3-6-08.citation: National Association of EMS Physicians. Essential Principles to Create an Equitable, Inclusive, and Diverse EMS Workforce and Work Environment. 2022:6.
EMS2026-1-3-6-08.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Use the video first because recruitment messaging should be separated from internal personnel policy.
options.B: Examine and revise policies affected by systemic racism, especially hiring and promotion practices, while continuing outreach.
options.C: Focus only on entry-level recruitment because promotion patterns are outside EMS medical oversight.
options.D: Wait for more applicant data because policy review is premature until a larger cohort applies.

EMS2026-1-3-6-08.rationale: Recruitment and retention efforts should examine policies affected by systemic racism, especially hiring and promotion practices. A treats messaging as sufficient, C ignores advancement barriers, and D delays action despite a visible pattern.
EMS2026-1-3-6-08.stem: An EMS agency has few applicants from neighborhoods it serves and even fewer promotions among clinicians from underrepresented groups. Leaders propose a new recruitment video but do not plan to examine hiring criteria, interview scoring, field-training assignment, or promotion pathways. Which improvement focus is most appropriate?
EMS2026-1-3-6-08.title: Recruitment And Systemic Racism

EMS2026-1-3-7-08.citation: National Association of EMS Physicians. Essential Principles to Create an Equitable, Inclusive, and Diverse EMS Workforce and Work Environment. 2022:4.
EMS2026-1-3-7-08.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Treat race as a biologic medication-response variable while separately tracking clinician implicit-bias training.
options.B: Race and ethnicity should be excluded from analysis because measuring them may worsen bias.
options.C: Race should be replaced by insurance status because structural factors cannot be evaluated in EMS datasets.
options.D: Treat race and ethnicity as social determinants, not biologic explanations.

EMS2026-1-3-7-08.rationale: Race and ethnicity are social determinants affected by structural racism and implicit bias; they should not be treated as biologic explanations for EMS care differences. A medicalizes inequity, B prevents measurement of disparities, and C substitutes one social variable while denying the need to examine race and ethnicity.
EMS2026-1-3-7-08.stem: An EMS quality committee finds differences in pain-medication administration by recorded race and ethnicity. One member proposes adding "biologic race" as a risk-adjustment variable before changing practice. Which framing is most appropriate?
EMS2026-1-3-7-08.title: Race, Ethnicity, And EMS Care Differences

EMS2026-1-4-3-4-4-01.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:163.
EMS2026-1-4-3-4-4-01.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Release the destination but not the diagnosis, because transport location is operational information rather than protected health information.
options.B: Confirm the diagnosis but not the name, because the student's identity is already apparent to bystanders.
options.C: Ask the teacher to approve a brief statement because school personnel can release student information during school events.
options.D: Provide only a general nonidentifying statement and route requests for patient-specific information through the agency's privacy process.

EMS2026-1-4-3-4-4-01.rationale: Most EMS operations must handle protected health information like other health care entities, so patient-specific details should not be disclosed to media without proper authority. A still releases potentially identifying health information, B treats public visibility as consent, and C gives the teacher authority the EMS agency cannot assume.
EMS2026-1-4-3-4-4-01.stem: At a school athletic event, a local reporter asks an EMS supervisor for the name, diagnosis, and destination hospital of an injured student. A teacher says the family is "fine with everyone knowing" but no parent is present. Which response best reflects Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) obligations for most EMS operations?
EMS2026-1-4-3-4-4-01.title: Health Privacy At A Public Scene
EMS2026-1-4-3-4-4-01.topic: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)

EMS2026-1-4-4-2-03.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:36-37.
EMS2026-1-4-4-2-03.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: It primarily improves ambulance availability by shortening the time crews spend in the emergency department.
options.B: It moves hospital preparation earlier, extending simultaneous processing and potentially shortening recognition-to-treatment intervals.
options.C: It replaces destination triage because field activation proves the patient should bypass the nearest hospital.
options.D: It removes the need for neurologist involvement because paramedic activation establishes the diagnosis.

EMS2026-1-4-4-2-03.rationale: Field activation lets receiving systems prepare before arrival, which is simultaneous processing. A is secondary at best, while C and D overstate what field activation determines.
EMS2026-1-4-4-2-03.stem: A stroke system previously activated the stroke team only after emergency-department physician evaluation. A new policy lets paramedics activate the team from the field when exam and time criteria are met. What is the main systems effect?
EMS2026-1-4-4-2-03.title: Field Activation As Simultaneous Processing

EMS2026-1-4-4-2-04.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:329.
EMS2026-1-4-4-2-04.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Tighten criteria until emergency-department discharge after trauma activation becomes rare, accepting a small rise in secondary transfer.
options.B: Accept some overtriage while tracking undertriage, specialty-resource use, transfers, diversion, and the effects of any criteria change.
options.C: Keep all current activations because low undertriage shows that the system's triage threshold is already optimal.
options.D: Use discharge rate as the primary trigger for criteria changes, then audit missed injuries, secondary transfers, diversion, and intensive-care admissions after the new threshold is stable.

EMS2026-1-4-4-2-04.rationale: Trauma systems tolerate some overtriage to avoid undertriage, but excessive overtriage can consume scarce trauma resources. A ignores the safety reason overtriage exists. C ignores resource burden. D treats discharge rate as the only target and risks increasing missed major injury.
EMS2026-1-4-4-2-04.stem: A regional trauma committee reviews a year of EMS triage. Undertriage is low, but a Level I trauma center reports frequent activation for patients discharged from the emergency department (ED). Rural hospitals argue that tightening field criteria may increase missed major injury. Which interpretation best fits trauma system oversight?
EMS2026-1-4-4-2-04.title: Trauma Overtriage Tradeoff

EMS2026-1-4-5-05.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:29-30.

options.A: Average response time is sufficient because system status management is designed to optimize aggregate performance.
options.B: Unit-hour utilization is sufficient because keeping ambulances busy shows that resources are deployed efficiently.
options.C: System status management must balance high- and low-volume coverage, peak and off-peak demand, workforce health, and economic stability.
options.D: Rural coverage should be excluded from deployment modeling because low call volume makes performance unstable.

EMS2026-1-4-5-05.rationale: SSM evaluation should not hide coverage, workforce, or economic harms behind an average response-time gain. A and B are incomplete, and D excludes a real service obligation.
EMS2026-1-4-5-05.stem: A system status management (SSM) posting algorithm improves 90th-percentile urban response time but increases rural uncovered hours, crew moves per shift, and late meal breaks. Leadership calls the model successful because the average response time improved. What should the medical director emphasize?
EMS2026-1-4-5-05.title: System Status Management Tradeoff

options.A: A voluntary five-year federal model for treatment in place, alternate destinations, reporting, and quality requirements.
options.B: A permanent federal payment rule requiring low-acuity 911 patients to use urgent care destinations when available.
options.C: A state licensure pathway allowing community paramedics to bill independently after documenting outcome measures.
options.D: A hospital diversion rule that pays EMS only when patients refuse transport after online medical consultation.

EMS2026-1-4-6-1-03.rationale: ET3 was a voluntary five-year Center for Medicare and Medicaid Innovation payment model supporting treatment in place and alternative destinations with reporting and quality requirements. B turns a model into a universal mandate, C removes oversight and reporting, and D misstates the payment and care pathways.
EMS2026-1-4-6-1-03.stem: A payer asks why an EMS agency's treatment-in-place pilot cannot be evaluated only by reduced transports. The medical director describes the Emergency Triage, Treat, and Transport (ET3) model. Which feature set best characterizes that model?
EMS2026-1-4-6-1-03.title: Emergency Triage, Treat, And Transport Model

options.A: Schedule home visits for all discharged patients, replace routine clinic follow-up, provide teleconsultation, and use transport only for unstable findings.
options.B: Send postdischarge symptoms back to the emergency department, track readmission rate, add nurse callbacks, and avoid nonconveyance decisions.
options.C: Limit community paramedics to medication inventory, social-needs screening, and referral lists generated by home health.
options.D: Use risk-selected in-home visits, teleconsultation, scheduled reassessment, and nonconveyance pathways under medical oversight.

EMS2026-1-4-6-2-03.rationale: Mobile integrated health programs may manage high-risk readmission patients through in-home visits, teleconsultation, scheduled follow-up, and nonconveyance workflows. A overreplaces usual care, B preserves the readmission cycle, and C narrows EMS to checklists.
EMS2026-1-4-6-2-03.stem: A health system asks EMS to help reduce readmissions among patients with heart failure, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and poor access to follow-up. The proposed EMS role is a once-monthly phone reminder after discharge. Which program design better reflects mobile integrated health treatment-in-place capability?
EMS2026-1-4-6-2-03.title: MIH Treatment-in-Place Design

EMS2026-1-5-1-09.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:256.

options.A: "EMS crews were checked and decontamination steps are in place. People near the original site with eye or breathing symptoms should move to fresh air and call the hotline."
options.B: "The ambulance-contamination rumor is false. Please stop sharing it while investigators determine what caused the reported symptoms."
options.C: "Monitoring has not shown spread beyond the original site. Residents with symptoms should avoid the area and follow public-health instructions."
options.D: "The investigation is continuing. Our next update at 6 PM will summarize confirmed exposure locations, symptoms to watch for, protective actions, and monitoring results."

EMS2026-1-5-1-09.rationale: The best statement gives actionable risk information while avoiding repetition of the false negative phrase as the main message. B repeats the rumor, C is close but speaks more about spread than EMS response and immediate symptom guidance, and D is transparent but less useful for exposed residents.
EMS2026-1-5-1-09.stem: After a chemical odor incident, a social media rumor claims that EMS ambulances carried contamination into neighborhoods. No evidence supports that claim, but several residents report eye irritation near the original site. Which public statement best avoids reinforcing the false framing while still giving useful risk information?
EMS2026-1-5-1-09.title: Risk Communication Without Repeating Rumor
EMS2026-1-5-1-09.topic: Public information and interacting with the media

options.D: Avoid public statistics because operational data are too technical for community audiences.

EMS2026-1-5-1-12.rationale: Public communication should make accurate information understandable. A and D reduce transparency and comprehension, while C risks misleading the public by cherry-picking favorable measures.
EMS2026-1-5-1-12.stem: An EMS agency wants to publish cardiac-arrest survival and response-time data. One draft uses a dense spreadsheet; another uses only the best-performing percentile. Which communication approach is best?
EMS2026-1-5-1-12.title: Public Reporting Without Misleading

options.A: State the protective action once, answer background questions, and rely on media outlets to repeat the instruction accurately.
options.B: Use different versions of the shelter message for television, radio, and social media to reduce message fatigue.
options.C: Repeat the same short protective action message throughout the briefing and return to it after questions.
options.D: Avoid repeating instructions until the chemical is confirmed because repeated instructions may seem alarmist.

EMS2026-1-5-3-05.rationale: Emergency risk communication uses repetition and redundancy so action messages survive stress, partial listening, and media editing. A buries the protective action, B risks inconsistent instructions, and D delays clear protective guidance.
EMS2026-1-5-3-05.stem: During a press briefing after a tanker crash, the EMS medical director must tell residents in two blocks to shelter in place, close windows, and monitor the city alert system. Reporters are asking about the crash cause and school reopening. What communication strategy is best?
EMS2026-1-5-3-05.title: Repeating Risk Messages

options.A: "The child is fine because the initial vital signs are normal and transport is precautionary."
options.B: "There is no reason for concern unless the hospital later reports a complication."
options.C: "We cannot comment, and families should assume nothing until the investigation, patient tracking, and hospital notifications are complete."
options.D: "The child is awake and receiving care; injuries can evolve, so evaluation and family notification are continuing."

EMS2026-1-5-3-08.rationale: Risk communication should avoid overreassuring about an individual patient's condition when deterioration or complications remain possible. A and B overpromise, while C withholds useful status and increases uncertainty.
EMS2026-1-5-3-08.stem: A bus crash injures several children. One child with abdominal pain is awake, has normal initial vital signs, and is being transported for evaluation. Reporters ask whether the child is "fine." Which response best follows emergency risk communication principles?
EMS2026-1-5-3-08.title: Avoiding Overreassurance

options.A: FirstNet was created by Public Law 112-96 to support nationwide public-safety broadband, but it does not eliminate the need for voice-radio interoperability planning.
options.B: FirstNet is the federal Next Generation 911 voice network, so it primarily replaces dispatcher call-taking and radio dispatch functions, while preserving legacy radio caches and dispatch center procedures during a staged technology transition.
options.C: FirstNet gives EMS priority voice channels on all commercial cellular towers, making mutual-aid radio caches mainly a legacy backup.
options.D: FirstNet is a hospital notification platform, so its main EMS value is receiving-facility alerting rather than field communications.

EMS2026-1-5-4-10.rationale: FirstNet is a public-safety broadband structure created under Public Law 112-96, not a full replacement for mission-critical voice systems. B confuses it with Next Generation 911. C overstates cellular broadband as a radio substitute. D narrows the program to hospital notification rather than broadband public-safety communications.
EMS2026-1-5-4-10.stem: A county is replacing aging radios and asks whether FirstNet funding means it can retire most mission-critical voice-radio interoperability work. Which statement best frames FirstNet for EMS communications planning?
EMS2026-1-5-4-10.title: FirstNet And Voice Interoperability

EMS2026-2-1-2-2-1-03.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:36-37.

options.A: Waveform end-tidal capnography after placement and during movement.
options.B: Tube condensation during ventilation combined with periodic pulse-oximetry trends.
options.C: Symmetric chest movement during bagging plus repeated visual reassessment.
options.D: Initial auscultation before transport followed by monitoring for chest-rise changes.

EMS2026-2-1-2-2-1-03.rationale: Waveform end-tidal capnography is the most accurate prehospital method for continuous tracheal tube verification and monitoring. Condensation, chest rise, and a single auscultation check are less reliable and do not provide continuous monitoring.
EMS2026-2-1-2-2-1-03.stem: A paramedic intubates a cardiac-arrest patient during transport. Bilateral chest rise is seen, breath sounds are difficult to hear over sirens, and condensation is present in the tube. Which verification method best supports continuous confirmation during transport?

EMS2026-2-1-2-2-3-01.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:31.
EMS2026-2-1-2-2-3-01.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: A Miller blade is placed in the vallecula, whereas a Macintosh blade directly lifts the epiglottis; field evidence favors Miller blades.
options.B: Macintosh uses the vallecula, Miller lifts the epiglottis directly, and neither is proven superior prehospital.
options.C: Both common blades are placed in the vallecula; the main decision is blade size rather than anatomy or field evidence.
options.D: Both common blades directly lift the epiglottis; superiority depends mainly on whether the patient is supine or seated.

EMS2026-2-1-2-2-3-01.rationale: The Macintosh blade is curved and placed in the vallecula, while the Miller blade is straight and directly lifts the epiglottis. Neither has demonstrated prehospital superiority. A reverses the landmarks, and C and D collapse distinct techniques into one.
EMS2026-2-1-2-2-3-01.stem: A paramedic is struggling with direct laryngoscopy in a manikin lab. The clinician places a curved blade directly beneath the epiglottis and lifts it, then says curved blades should be preferred because they are more reliable in the field. Which correction is most accurate?
EMS2026-2-1-2-2-3-01.title: Direct Laryngoscopy Blade Technique

EMS2026-2-1-2-2-4-04.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:32.

options.A: Video laryngoscopy eliminates the need for post-intubation tube confirmation in most field settings.
options.B: Shared airway view and recorded attempts for review, education, and quality improvement.
options.C: Video laryngoscopy is the main quality tool because improved glottic view reduces the need for case review.
options.D: Video laryngoscopy prevents aspiration during drug-assisted intubation by improving the glottic view.

EMS2026-2-1-2-2-4-04.rationale: Video laryngoscopy can support team performance and quality improvement because others can see the view and some devices record attempts for later review. A removes required tube confirmation, C overstates benefit, and D attributes aspiration prevention to the device.
EMS2026-2-1-2-2-4-04.stem: An EMS agency is deciding whether to use video laryngoscopy only as a rescue device or as part of its quality-improvement program. Which feature best supports the quality-improvement argument?
EMS2026-2-1-2-2-4-04.title: Video Laryngoscopy For Team Performance

options.A: Diphenhydramine because absence of urticaria makes isolated bronchospasm more likely than anaphylaxis.
options.B: Corticosteroid because hypotension after medication exposure is most likely delayed inflammatory shock.
options.C: Intramuscular epinephrine because anaphylactic shock may occur without rash and should not wait for adjuncts.
options.D: Nebulized albuterol because wheezing is the most immediately reversible threat and epinephrine can follow if hives appear.

EMS2026-2-1-3-2-02.rationale: Anaphylaxis can present without rash, and hypotension with airway and gastrointestinal features after exposure requires prompt intramuscular epinephrine. A and B are adjunct-focused and delay the key treatment, while D treats bronchospasm but misses the systemic shock state.
EMS2026-2-1-3-2-02.stem: A 43-year-old develops wheezing, throat tightness, vomiting, and hypotension 8 minutes after receiving an antibiotic infusion at home. Skin is cool and pale, and no rash is visible. The crew has epinephrine, diphenhydramine, corticosteroid, and nebulized albuterol. Which first medication choice is best?
EMS2026-2-1-3-2-02.title: Anaphylactic Shock Without Rash

EMS2026-2-1-3-3-3-04.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:86.

options.A: Emergency medications should be withheld until peripheral or central venous access is obtained.
options.B: Intraosseous medications require double dosing because marrow circulation substantially lowers bioavailability.
options.C: Most emergency medications can be given intraosseously with central-venous-like bioavailability.
options.D: Intraosseous delivery is appropriate only for fluids because vasoactive medications cause marrow necrosis.

EMS2026-2-1-3-3-3-04.rationale: Most emergency medications can be delivered intraosseously, with bioavailability and time to peak concentration similar to central venous administration. A delays care, B invents a routine dose adjustment, and D incorrectly limits intraosseous use to fluids.
EMS2026-2-1-3-3-3-04.stem: A pulseless adult has no peripheral access after two attempts. An intraosseous line is placed and flushes easily. The team hesitates to give epinephrine through it, believing drug delivery will be delayed compared with central venous access. Which statement is best?

options.A: Reassess the site and consider replacement if aspiration, flush, or device stability becomes abnormal.
options.B: Use the line mainly for medications if pressure-assisted flow remains inadequate despite confirmed patency.
options.C: Wait for spontaneous flow after the medullary pressure equilibrates, then reassess in 10 minutes.
options.D: Secure the line, confirm patency, and use a pressure bag or manual push to infuse fluids effectively.

EMS2026-2-1-3-3-3-05.rationale: Intraosseous infusion often requires pressure or manual push, with line security and patency checks. A overinterprets poor gravity flow, B falsely limits fluids, and C delays needed resuscitation.
EMS2026-2-1-3-3-3-05.stem: An intraosseous line is placed in a hypotensive child. The line flushes and aspirates marrow, but gravity flow is minimal. Which next action best addresses the expected limitation of intraosseous access?
EMS2026-2-1-3-3-3-05.title: Intraosseous Infusion Flow
EMS2026-2-1-3-3-3-05.topic: Placement of intraosseous lines (adult and pediatric)

options.A: Launch on supervisor vehicles only, require online physician approval for each transfusion, and address cooler failures through routine chart review.
options.B: Delay launch until the blood-bank agreement, cold-chain monitoring, unit reconciliation, training, wastage tracking, and adverse-event review are operational.
options.C: Start with air-medical intercept for the first quarter, using ground crews to collect activation, transport-time, and outcome data before carrying blood themselves.
options.D: Launch with crystalloid-first resuscitation and reserve blood for patients who remain hypotensive after transport begins and hospital notification is complete.

EMS2026-2-1-3-4-01.rationale: A prehospital blood program depends on more than indications and dosing; storage, blood-bank partnership, reconciliation, training, wastage, and adverse-event review must work before launch. A adds physician approval but leaves core system failures unresolved. C may be a reasonable interim access strategy but does not make the agency ready to carry blood. D delays blood in the patients most likely to need it and still lacks transfusion-program controls.
EMS2026-2-1-3-4-01.stem: A suburban advanced life support (ALS) agency is preparing to carry low-titer group O whole blood for hemorrhagic shock. In tabletop testing, one cooler exceeds its temperature range during a long standby, unused units are not reconciled with the blood bank, and a crew is unsure where to report a suspected transfusion reaction. The draft protocol already lists indications and dose. Which launch decision is most defensible?
EMS2026-2-1-3-4-01.title: Blood Product Program Readiness

EMS2026-2-2-1-1-04.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:258.

options.A: Convert to durable hemorrhage control, such as a tourniquet or pressure dressing, then move immediately to transport.
options.B: Load now while the firefighter maintains manual pressure because every on-scene intervention worsens severe-trauma time goals.
options.C: Start vascular access and give tranexamic acid before movement because shock treatment should precede transport.
options.D: Perform a full secondary survey before movement because proximal extremity trauma can hide pelvic and abdominal injury.

EMS2026-2-2-1-1-04.rationale: Minimizing scene time does not mean transporting with uncontrolled or hand-dependent life-threatening bleeding. A creates durable hemorrhage control before movement. B risks loss of control, while C and D add delay before the immediate preventable death problem is stabilized.
EMS2026-2-2-1-1-04.stem: A trauma crew is 9 minutes from the trauma center with a patient who has a mangled thigh, systolic blood pressure of 76 mm Hg, and heavy arterial bleeding controlled only while a firefighter maintains direct pressure. The stretcher is at the patient's side. What should the crew do first?
EMS2026-2-2-1-1-04.title: Scene Time Versus Hemorrhage Control
EMS2026-2-2-1-1-04.topic: Goal of minimizing scene time for severe trauma

options.A: Normal physiology places him below anatomic triage criteria, so the closest emergency department is preferred.
options.B: Suspected unstable pelvic injury is an anatomic criterion supporting trauma-center transport despite normal physiology.
options.C: The rollover mechanism is the controlling criterion; anatomic findings matter only after hospital imaging.
options.D: Binder placement corrects the triage criterion, so trauma-center need depends only on deterioration during transport.

EMS2026-2-2-1-3-03.rationale: Field triage anatomic criteria can support trauma-center transport even when physiology is initially normal. Suspected pelvic fracture or instability is one of those criteria. A ignores anatomic criteria, C overweights mechanism and delays the field triage decision until imaging, and D treats binder placement as if it removes the underlying high-risk injury pattern.
EMS2026-2-2-1-3-03.stem: After a farm-equipment rollover, a 35-year-old is awake, normotensive, and has no respiratory distress. He has severe pelvic pain and gross pelvic instability on careful assessment, so crews apply a pelvic binder. The closest community hospital is 12 minutes away, and the trauma center is 28 minutes away. Which triage interpretation is best?
EMS2026-2-2-1-3-03.title: Unstable Pelvis Field Triage

EMS2026-2-2-1-4-2-04.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:258.

options.A: Apply routine rigid spinal motion restriction for torso gunshot wounds when the crew cannot complete a neurologic assessment.
options.B: No routine spinal restriction unless neurologic deficit, obtundation, or another concern exists.
options.C: Apply a cervical collar but not a board when neck trajectory, neurologic symptoms, or examination limits raise concern.
options.D: Prioritize spinal packaging after hemorrhage control when blunt mechanism or neurologic findings create a separate indication.

EMS2026-2-2-1-4-2-04.rationale: Routine spinal motion restriction is not required for most gunshot wounds, except selected cases such as neurologic deficit, obtundation with high suspicion, or other specific concern. A and C make penetration itself the indication, while D dangerously reverses trauma priorities.
EMS2026-2-2-1-4-2-04.stem: A patient has an isolated gunshot wound to the lateral chest, is awake, moves all extremities, has no neurologic deficit, and needs rapid hemorrhage and respiratory assessment. Which spinal-motion approach is best?
EMS2026-2-2-1-4-2-04.title: Spinal Restriction In Gunshot Wounds

options.A: Delay tourniquet placement until the last dose and international normalized ratio are known because anticoagulant status changes bleeding-control thresholds.
options.B: Continue hemorrhage control with pressure or tourniquet as needed while identifying anticoagulant use for handoff.
options.C: Avoid tourniquet use because anticoagulated patients have a higher risk of limb ischemia.
options.D: Focus on reversal-agent availability because field hemorrhage control is unlikely to work in anticoagulated patients.

EMS2026-2-2-1-5-05.rationale: Anticoagulant history matters, but it does not replace immediate bleeding control. A delays control, C is inaccurate, and D overemphasizes reversal before field hemorrhage control.
EMS2026-2-2-1-5-05.stem: A patient on warfarin has a deep laceration with brisk bleeding. Direct pressure slows but does not stop bleeding, and the hospital asks about last dose and indication. What should the crew prioritize?
EMS2026-2-2-1-5-05.title: Anticoagulants Without Losing Hemorrhage Priorities

options.B: Tape the limb tightly to a pole at the deformity so the fracture cannot move during evacuation or transfer.
options.C: Use clothing alone because improvised rigid materials create unacceptable pressure-injury risk.
options.D: Straighten the deformity completely, place rigid material directly on skin, and secure both joints tightly before checking pulses.

EMS2026-2-2-5-1-1-03.rationale: Basic splinting protects skin and soft tissue, uses rigidity to immobilize, and secures the splint without compromising circulation. B and D create pressure and circulation risks, while C gives up the immobilizing function.
EMS2026-2-2-5-1-1-03.stem: A wilderness crew must splint a painful mid-forearm deformity using limited supplies. They have trekking poles, clothing, elastic wraps, and tape. Distal pulse and sensation are intact. Which construction plan best reflects basic splint principles?

options.A: Attempt immediate reduction because restoring anatomy is the fastest way to reduce pain during a short transport.
options.B: Attempt reduction only after two failed splinting positions because intact distal function makes the procedure low risk.
options.C: Request a higher-level unit at the scene for analgesia before moving, because reduction comfort should be addressed before emergency department arrival.
options.D: Immobilize for comfort and transport for monitored analgesia-assisted reduction unless distal neurovascular status worsens.

EMS2026-2-2-5-1-2-05.rationale: With intact distal neurovascular status, short transport, and inadequate analgesia/monitoring, deferring reduction is reasonable. A treats pain relief as enough justification, B invents a splinting threshold, and C delays definitive care for a procedure that can be performed shortly in the ED.
EMS2026-2-2-5-1-2-05.stem: A 34-year-old has an anterior shoulder dislocation after a fall. Radial pulse, sensation, and motor function are intact. The patient has severe pain, the crew has no analgesic or sedating medication in this response tier, and the emergency department (ED) is 9 minutes away. What is the best medical-control recommendation?
EMS2026-2-2-5-1-2-05.title: Reduction Deferred For Analgesia

options.A: Clamp visible vessels, remove loose contaminated tissue, wrap the stump dry, and leave splinting for the hospital.
options.B: Scrub the stump aggressively, apply dry gauze, loosen the tourniquet for perfusion checks, and avoid fracture splinting.
options.C: Gently clean, cover with saline-moistened sterile gauze, avoid clamping or debridement, and splint as a fracture.
options.D: Pack the stump tightly with hemostatic gauze, irrigate only after hospital arrival, and keep the limb mobile for reassessment.

EMS2026-2-2-8-1-04.rationale: After bleeding control, traumatic amputation stump care includes gentle cleaning, saline-moistened sterile covering, avoiding vessel clamping or manual debridement, and splinting as if an underlying fracture is present. A and B cause tissue or vascular harm, while D overpacks and fails to immobilize.
EMS2026-2-2-8-1-04.stem: A worker has a partial traumatic forearm amputation. A tourniquet has controlled life-threatening bleeding, and the stump is grossly contaminated but not actively spurting. Transport is underway. Which stump-care plan is best?
EMS2026-2-2-8-1-04.title: Traumatic Amputation Stump Care

options.A: Give high-flow oxygen to both patients because short prehospital exposure cannot cause harm.

options.D: Use oxygen primarily for comfort because pulse oximetry does not change prehospital management.

EMS2026-2-3-1-3-01.rationale: Oxygen should be targeted to hypoxemia, distress, or compromise rather than given reflexively. A overuses oxygen, C undertreats this dyspneic hypoxemic patient, and D reframes oxygen as comfort care rather than physiologic treatment.
EMS2026-2-3-1-3-01.stem: A patient with pneumonia is dyspneic with oxygen saturation of 91%. A second patient with renal colic has normal work of breathing, warm skin, and oxygen saturation of 98% but asks for oxygen because it feels reassuring. Which oxygen approach is best?

EMS2026-2-3-1-6-1-05.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Pneumothorax shrinkage at altitude, with pneumonia, pain control, and atelectasis becoming the main in-flight issues.
options.B: Full cabin pressurization, which removes gas-expansion risk in fixed-wing medical transport when the aircraft is medically configured.
options.C: Pneumothorax expansion limited to unpressurized rotor-wing flight rather than fixed-wing transport with a pressurized cabin.
options.D: Trapped pleural gas expansion during air transport, especially with higher altitude or lower cabin pressure.

EMS2026-2-3-1-6-1-05.rationale: Air transport can expand pneumothorax because gas volume changes with altitude and cabin pressure. A reverses the physiology, B overstates cabin pressurization, and C incorrectly limits the concern to rotor-wing flight.

The planned flight includes higher altitude segments, and the cabin will be pressurized but not to sea level. The patient is stable on low-flow oxygen. Which risk should be addressed before accepting the flight?
EMS2026-2-3-1-6-1-05.title: Pneumothorax During Air Transport

options.C: Build training, credentialing, procedural standards, reassessment expectations, and handoff requirements before implementation.
options.D: Reject the procedure categorically because finger thoracostomy is inappropriate outside physician-performed emergency department care.

EMS2026-2-3-1-6-2-03.rationale: Finger thoracostomy may be reasonable in selected systems, but it requires more than an indication list. A and B leave competence and follow-up undefined, while D overstates a categorical prohibition.

The draft protocol names indications and equipment but does not specify training models, competency sign-off, sterile technique, reassessment for reaccumulation, or receiving-hospital handoff. EMS2026-2-3-10-2-01.citation: Pinto-Villalba RS, Balseca-Arellano D, Leon-Rojas JE. Septic shock in the prehospital setting: a scoping review. Scand J Trauma Resusc Emerg Med. 2024;32:113.

options.A: Start broad-spectrum antibiotics early after reliable septic-shock identification under protocol.
options.B: Give antibiotics to every febrile hypotensive patient before confirming a sepsis syndrome or contraindication.
options.C: Use crystalloid response as the only field treatment because antibiotics are not time-sensitive until source control.
options.D: Withhold antibiotics until hospital cultures are obtained, even when field identification and protocols are reliable.

EMS2026-2-3-10-2-01.rationale: Early broad-spectrum antibiotics are recommended after septic shock diagnosis, and prehospital use may help when identification and protocols are reliable. B removes the needed safeguards, C treats fluids as a substitute for antimicrobial therapy, and D delays a time-sensitive treatment despite a mature protocol.
EMS2026-2-3-10-2-01.stem: A regional EMS system has validated sepsis screening, medical-director-approved antibiotics, online backup, and average transport times of 45 minutes. A hospital leader asks whether field antibiotics should be delayed until emergency department cultures are drawn. What is the best medical-oversight position?
EMS2026-2-3-10-2-01.title: Prehospital Antibiotics in Septic Shock

EMS2026-2-3-10-2-05.citation: Pinto-Villalba RS, Balseca-Arellano D, Leon-Rojas JE. Septic shock in the prehospital setting: a scoping review. Scand J Trauma Resusc Emerg Med. 2024;32:113.
EMS2026-2-3-10-2-05.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Support tissue perfusion with a general mean arterial pressure target of at least 65 mm Hg, considering selected chronic hypertension patients.
options.B: Target systolic pressure above 80 mm Hg because lower sepsis pressures reduce vasopressor exposure during long transports and serial reassessments.
options.C: Continue fluids until mottling resolves before considering vasopressors, destination capability, or reassessment of perfusion.
options.D: Use mental-status normalization as the endpoint because mean arterial pressure is unreliable in chronic hypertension and prolonged transport.

EMS2026-2-3-10-2-05.rationale: Septic shock hemodynamic support targets tissue perfusion, generally mean arterial pressure at least 65 mm Hg, with higher targets considered in chronic hypertension. B sets too low a target, C uses one endpoint in isolation, and D discards a central perfusion measure.
EMS2026-2-3-10-2-05.stem: A patient with suspected sepsis remains confused and mottled after an initial fluid bolus. Blood pressure is 82/44 mm Hg, mean arterial pressure is 57 mm Hg, and the patient has chronic hypertension. Which hemodynamic goal best fits sepsis care principles?
EMS2026-2-3-10-2-05.title: Septic Shock Perfusion Target

EMS2026-2-3-11-1-3-04.citation: Van Gelder CM. Protection of EMS personnel from occupationally acquired infections. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:88.

options.A: Contact precautions focused on rash drainage and stretcher-surface contamination.
options.B: Airborne precautions for possible rubeola pending confirmation.
options.C: Droplet precautions only because cough is present but lower-airway disease is absent.
options.D: Standard precautions only because the rash is generalized rather than vesicular.

EMS2026-2-3-11-1-3-04.rationale: Fever with cough, coryza, and maculopapular rash is a measles-compatible syndrome requiring airborne precautions pending confirmation. A would fit selected draining skin conditions, C underprotects against airborne transmission, and D treats a classic risk pattern as routine.
EMS2026-2-3-11-1-3-04.stem: During a measles outbreak, EMS evaluates an unimmunized college student with fever, cough, coryza, conjunctival irritation, and a generalized maculopapular rash. He is not vomiting, has no diarrhea, and is breathing comfortably. Which initial infection-control approach best fits the presentation?

EMS2026-2-3-11-6-2-02.citation: MacDonald RD. Infectious and communicable diseases. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:219.

options.A: Give prophylaxis to every employee on duty that day because meningococcal exposure is too high risk to stratify.
options.B: Wait for final cultures before gathering crew details because prophylaxis decisions should not begin from suspected cases.
options.C: Ask the hospital to contact each crew member directly because EMS infection-control officers should not collect clinical exposure details.
options.D: Gather case-specific exposure details to determine which prehospital personnel truly need evaluation or prophylaxis.

EMS2026-2-3-11-6-2-02.rationale: The infection-control officer should gather case-specific details to determine which EMS personnel need evaluation or postexposure prophylaxis. A over-treats without exposure stratification, B delays the process unnecessarily, and C fragments the EMS agency's exposure follow-up role.
EMS2026-2-3-11-6-2-02.stem: A hospital notifies an EMS infection-control officer that a transported patient may have meningococcal disease. The crew included a driver, a treating paramedic who performed airway suctioning, and a trainee who briefly carried equipment. Which first step best supports appropriate postexposure prophylaxis decisions?

EMS2026-2-3-11-6-2-02.topic: Infection control liaison between hospital and EMS agency

EMS2026-2-3-12-3-1-04.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:473.

options.A: Increase distance, keep responders out of the exit path, reduce weapon access if feasible, and use one calm communicator.

options.D: Have multiple responders ask questions so the patient can choose which voice feels safest.

EMS2026-2-3-12-3-1-04.rationale: De-escalation starts with scene setup: distance, egress, reducing weapon access, and calm single-channel communication. B and D increase stimulation and perceived threat, while C leaves a correctable hazard in place.
EMS2026-2-3-12-3-1-04.stem: An intoxicated patient with paranoid statements is standing in a kitchen. Loose knives are on the counter, two responders are between the patient and the exit, and a supervisor is asking rapid questions from 3 feet away. Which adjustment best reduces violence risk?

EMS2026-2-3-13-3-1-03.citation: Moy HP, Benson RT II. High-altitude illnesses. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:411.

options.A: High-altitude cerebral edema, because headache after rapid ascent is sufficient to indicate brain swelling.
options.B: Dehydration only, because acute altitude illness requires abnormal lung findings or oxygen desaturation.
options.C: Acute mountain sickness with headache and associated symptoms but no neurologic deficits.
options.D: Carbon monoxide exposure, because sleep disturbance and nausea exclude altitude-related illness.

EMS2026-2-3-13-3-1-03.rationale: Acute mountain sickness is a clinical diagnosis with headache plus symptoms such as fatigue, nausea, dizziness, anorexia, or sleep disturbance, without focal neurologic deficits or ataxia. A escalates despite a normal neurologic exam, B requires findings not needed for diagnosis, and D ignores the altitude context.
EMS2026-2-3-13-3-1-03.stem: A hiker who rapidly ascended to altitude has headache, nausea, poor sleep, and fatigue. The patient is oriented, walks normally, and has no focal neurologic deficit. Which interpretation is best?

EMS2026-2-3-13-3-3-02.citation: Moy HP, Benson RT II. High-altitude illnesses. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:412.

options.A: Give a sedating antiemetic and continue ascent if the headache improves overnight.
options.B: Begin descent immediately because any nausea at altitude indicates high-altitude cerebral edema.
options.C: Stop ascent, treat symptoms, and consider acetazolamide while assessing for worsening.
options.D: Administer supplemental oxygen for 10 minutes and clear ascent if oxygen saturation rises.

EMS2026-2-3-13-3-3-02.rationale: Mild acute mountain sickness is managed by halting ascent and allowing acclimatization, with symptom treatment and acetazolamide as appropriate. A risks respiratory depression and ignores acclimatization, B overcalls mild illness as cerebral edema, and D uses transient oxygen response as clearance for continued ascent.
EMS2026-2-3-13-3-3-02.stem: A hiker at 10,500 feet has headache, anorexia, and mild nausea on the first night after rapid ascent. He is walking normally, has no confusion, ataxia, dyspnea at rest, cough, or focal neurologic deficit, and the group planned to continue to 12,000 feet in the morning. Which plan is best?

EMS2026-2-3-13-4-4-01.citation: Couturier K, Husain I. Diving injury. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:422-424.

options.C: Increased nitrogen partial pressure causing intoxication at depth.
options.D: Immersion pulmonary edema from cold-water vasoconstriction and increased afterload.

EMS2026-2-3-13-4-4-01.rationale: Nitrogen narcosis is an at-depth intoxicating effect from increased nitrogen partial pressure, explained by Dalton's law. A describes decompression physiology, B describes barotrauma, and D describes a respiratory syndrome rather than depth-related cognition that improves with ascent.
EMS2026-2-3-13-4-4-01.stem: A diver at depth becomes euphoric, inattentive, and slow to respond. The symptoms improve as the diver ascends to a shallower depth. There is no joint pain, rash, dyspnea, or neurologic deficit after surfacing. Which mechanism best explains the underwater symptoms?

options.C: The pulseless apneic patient, because lightning arrest can respond to immediate ventilation and compressions.
options.D: The patient with leg pain, because traumatic injury is easier to stabilize before transport resources arrive.

EMS2026-2-3-13-5-1-01.rationale: Lightning mass-casualty incidents are an exception to ordinary triage patterns: arrest patients may receive highest priority because respiratory arrest and dysrhythmia can be rapidly reversible with immediate support. A, B, and D apply more routine triage instincts to a lightning-specific problem.

options.D: Use the non-PCI hospital for fibrinolysis when timely PCI cannot be achieved, with planned transfer to a PCI-capable center.

options.D: Nebulized bronchodilator therapy first because wheezing and crackles both indicate obstructive airway disease.

options.D: Remain on scene for delivery while tracking fetal heart tones, then transport if perfusion worsens or delivery stalls.

EMS2026-2-3-7-2-2-02.rationale: Some high-risk delivery complications cannot be definitively resolved prehospital and require temporizing measures plus rapid transport. B and D overcommit to field delivery; C uses a movement strategy that does not address malpresentation risk.
EMS2026-2-3-7-2-2-02.stem: A rural crew is called for a preterm delivery with a limb presenting at the introitus. The closest obstetric hospital is 35 minutes away. The mother is awake, contractions continue, and fetal heart tones are present. Which EMS strategy is best?

EMS2026-2-3-8-6-05.rationale: Progressive venom effects make antivenom-capable destination planning and poison-center or toxicology consultation important when transport time is acceptable. A may be right for instability but risks avoidable delay here, B delays care for species confirmation, and D places antivenom logistics ahead of patient movement and receiving-facility coordination.
EMS2026-2-3-8-6-05.stem: A 29-year-old is bitten by a suspected rattlesnake. He is stable but has progressive swelling past the ankle. The nearest hospital is 12 minutes away and has no antivenom agreement; a tertiary center 35 minutes away has toxicology support and antivenom access. What is the best destination recommendation?
EMS2026-2-3-8-6-05.title: Envenomation Destination Planning

EMS2026-2-4-12-1-02.citation: O'Connor RE, McStay C. Approach to the geriatric patient. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:405.

options.A: Treat nontransport as high risk and consider medical and social condition, online medical direction, primary care or family contact, and referral pathways.
options.B: Accept refusal once she denies pain because lift-assist calls without injury are social-service matters rather than EMS medical events.
options.C: Transport against her will because repeat lift assists prove inability to make decisions independently.
options.D: Document the lift only and leave referral decisions to the next caller because social factors are outside EMS care.

EMS2026-2-4-12-1-02.rationale: Older-adult refusal or nontransport after a lift assist is high risk and should consider medical and social condition, consultation, and referral. B and D underrespond to risk, while C overrules autonomy without a capacity basis.
EMS2026-2-4-12-1-02.stem: EMS responds for an 84-year-old who fell while transferring from bed to chair. She denies injury and wants a lift assist only, but the apartment has no food visible, medications are scattered, and she has called twice this month. Which refusal or nontransport approach is best?
EMS2026-2-4-12-1-02.title: Lift Assist Refusal Risk

EMS2026-2-4-13-03.citation: Hong T, Case V, Farcas AM, et al. Caring for transgender and gender diverse prehospital patients: a NAEMSP position statement and resource document. Prehosp Emerg Care. 2025;29(3):305. doi:10.1080/10903127.2024.2411723.
