options.A: Use only the identification name in conversation and handoff so documentation and verbal care match exactly.
options.B: Avoid names and pronouns during care and handoff because staff may disagree about what to use.
options.C: Use the patient's identified name and pronouns during care and handoff, while preserving required identifying information.
options.D: Ask the receiving hospital to decide which name and pronouns should be used after registration is complete.

EMS2026-2-4-13-03.rationale: EMS clinicians should use patient-identified names and pronouns during direct care, handoffs, and documentation, even when these differ from identification documents, while preserving necessary identifying information. A and D prioritize systems over respectful care, and B avoids a necessary communication task.
EMS2026-2-4-13-03.stem: EMS is caring for a transgender patient whose identification lists a different name and sex marker than the patient provides. The patient is awake, anxious, and being transferred to the emergency department. Which practice is most appropriate?
EMS2026-2-4-13-03.title: Names and Pronouns in Prehospital Care

EMS2026-2-4-13-05.citation: Hong T, Case V, Farcas AM, et al. Caring for transgender and gender diverse prehospital patients: a NAEMSP position statement and resource document. Prehosp Emerg Care. 2025;29(3):308-309. doi:10.1080/10903127.2024.2411723.
EMS2026-2-4-13-05.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Ask only about abdominal pain and transport, because anatomy questions risk stigmatizing the patient and can wait for the emergency department.
options.B: Obtain a full transition, hormone, sexual, and surgical history on every transgender patient because hidden anatomy is always clinically relevant.
options.C: Ask clinically relevant anatomy and pregnancy-capable-organ questions while using the patient's name and pronouns and avoiding unrelated transition details.
options.D: Ask the receiving hospital to clarify anatomy and gender-affirming treatment because EMS should document only the patient's stated gender identity.

EMS2026-2-4-13-05.rationale: This complaint makes reproductive and abdominal anatomy clinically relevant, but the history should remain targeted and respectful. A omits needed diagnostic information, B is overbroad and intrusive, and D shifts relevant EMS assessment to the hospital.
EMS2026-2-4-13-05.stem: A transgender man with a uterus calls for severe right-lower-quadrant pain, vomiting, and near-syncope. He takes testosterone, has not had abdominal surgery, and is worried responders will focus on his gender rather than the pain. Which history approach is best?
EMS2026-2-4-13-05.title: Clinically Relevant Gender-Affirming History

options.A: Excellent pediatric bag-mask ventilation, with pediatric-specific training and a rescue plan before any advanced airway remains in scope.
options.B: Use pediatric endotracheal intubation as the primary airway endpoint and train around first-pass tube placement.
options.C: Use pediatric surgical-airway rescue training and early cricothyrotomy as the main backup for failed ventilation.
options.D: Adult airway sequencing with pediatric dose cards and chart review, because pediatric differences are mainly size and medication dose.

EMS2026-2-4-5-1-04.rationale: Pediatric advanced airway management is low-frequency and high-risk; excellent bag-mask ventilation is foundational. B overprioritizes intubation, C is rarely the first pediatric rescue option, and D understates pediatric-specific training needs.
EMS2026-2-4-5-1-04.stem: A rural EMS agency has almost no pediatric intubation attempts. The medical director is deciding whether to keep pediatric endotracheal intubation in scope. Which capability must be excellent regardless of that decision?
EMS2026-2-4-5-1-04.title: Pediatric Airway Program Threshold

options.A: Confirm battery charge, ventilator mode, circuit tubing, and oxygen cylinder pressure before changing the airway plan.
options.B: Treat bronchospasm, mucus plugging below the tracheostomy, aspiration pneumonia, and chronic lung disease first because lower-airway disease can mimic equipment failure.
options.C: Rapidly assess for dislodgement, obstruction, pneumothorax or other patient pathology, and equipment malfunction (DOPE).
options.D: Assume anxiety or breath-holding until caregiver history proves a mechanical airway or ventilator problem.

EMS2026-2-4-5-6-04.rationale: The clinical pattern requires a rapid dislodgement, obstruction, pneumothorax or patient pathology, and equipment malfunction (DOPE) assessment. A checks some equipment issues but is too narrow. B may become relevant later but does not match the immediate high-pressure and poor-ventilation pattern. D ignores objective evidence of a ventilation problem.
EMS2026-2-4-5-6-04.stem: A ventilator-dependent child with a tracheostomy becomes cyanotic during transport. After the stretcher is moved, the portable ventilator alarms high pressure; suction passes with resistance and bag-valve ventilation through the tracheostomy gives poor chest rise. Which immediate troubleshooting frame is best?
EMS2026-2-4-5-6-04.title: Technology-Dependent Child Deterioration
EMS2026-2-4-5-6-04.topic: Children with special healthcare needs (e.g., technology dependent)

EMS2026-2-4-6-1-05.citation: Mancera M, Lohmeier M, Shah MN. Approach to the geriatric patient. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:464.
EMS2026-2-4-6-1-05.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Pad kyphosis, minimize backboard time, protect skin, and prevent hypothermia.
options.B: Place the patient on a padded long board with kyphosis support and keep it for the entire transport.
options.C: Avoid blankets until the trauma center examination because warming can hide skin tears and pressure injury.
options.D: Use a rigid board for the entire transport because age alone makes occult spinal instability likely.

EMS2026-2-4-6-1-05.rationale: Older adults may need padding for kyphosis, backboard-minimizing protocols, skin protection, and temperature control. B forces painful malalignment, C ignores thermoregulation and skin risk, and D makes age alone the reason for prolonged rigid immobilization.
EMS2026-2-4-6-1-05.stem: An 86-year-old with kyphosis falls down three stairs and has hip pain but no neurologic deficit. The crew plans transport on the ambulance cot after brief extrication. Which packaging approach best anticipates geriatric harms?
EMS2026-2-4-6-1-05.title: Geriatric Trauma Packaging

EMS2026-2-4-9-1-05.citation: Dees L. Family and bystanders. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:546.

options.A: Medical-director-approved exceptions for selected vulnerable patients with infection-control precautions.
options.B: Allow family accompaniment after symptom screening and masking whenever patient anxiety is severe.
options.C: Maintain a blanket no-accompaniment rule until outbreak conditions improve, because exceptions may undermine public messaging.
options.D: Replace family accompaniment with written discharge instructions because the main problem is post-transport communication.

EMS2026-2-4-9-1-05.rationale: Quarantine and isolation can worsen anxiety and separate patients from family; medical directors should define operational policies and exceptions for vulnerable patients when accompaniment is restricted. B ignores infection-control constraints, C ignores vulnerable-patient harms, and D does not address distress during transport.
EMS2026-2-4-9-1-05.stem: During a contagious respiratory outbreak, a transport policy prohibits all family members from riding with patients. Crews report worsening distress among children, patients with dementia, and patients with communication barriers. Which policy revision best balances infection control with vulnerable-patient needs?
EMS2026-2-4-9-1-05.title: Quarantine And Family Accompaniment

EMS2026-3-1-2-1-08.citation: Bigham BL, Nolan B, Patterson PD. Patient safety culture. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:418.

options.A: Continue the pathway but review only unit availability, transport reduction, and patient-satisfaction data because those were the approved goals.
options.B: Treat the pathway as safety-sensitive, refining criteria, follow-up linkage, adverse-event review, and outcome monitoring before expansion.
options.C: Suspend nonconveyance temporarily while reviewing every delayed return, then restart only if no preventable deterioration is found.
options.D: Convert clinic referrals into signed refusals with follow-up instructions so later deterioration is tracked outside pathway performance.

EMS2026-3-1-2-1-08.rationale: Alternate-disposition programs can improve operations, but they remain clinically safety-sensitive and require outcome linkage, adverse-event review, follow-up reliability, and criteria oversight. A overweights efficiency. C overreacts without system review. D misclassifies a pathway decision as refusal documentation.
EMS2026-3-1-2-1-08.stem: A 911 alternate-disposition pathway reduces emergency department transports by 18% and improves unit availability. Outcome linkage is incomplete; chart review finds one patient returned in shock after clinic referral, and several home follow-up calls could not be completed. Which oversight response is best?
EMS2026-3-1-2-1-08.title: Nonconveyance Oversight

EMS2026-3-1-2-3-06.citation: Bigham BL, Nolan B, Patterson PD. Patient safety culture. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:419.

options.A: EMS clinicians usually know patient outcomes, but agencies lack software to attach those outcomes, hospital notes, and safety reports to run records.
options.B: Hospitals identify EMS adverse events reliably, but EMS agencies often reject external reports as non-actionable or outside prehospital control.
options.C: EMS adverse events are rare enough that dedicated surveillance is less useful than individual chart review and complaint investigation.
options.D: EMS may lose follow-up, use multiple hospitals, and not be recognized as part of the event chain.

EMS2026-3-1-2-3-06.rationale: EMS adverse events are underrecognized partly because crews lose follow-up, transport to multiple hospitals, hospitals may not know EMS's contribution, and surveillance systems are fragmented. A and B overstate existing feedback, while C assumes low event frequency rather than measurement difficulty.
EMS2026-3-1-2-3-06.stem: A medical director notices that hospital safety reports rarely identify EMS contributors, even after cases where prehospital care may have affected outcome. Which explanation best identifies a structural reason EMS adverse events are underrecognized?
EMS2026-3-1-2-3-06.title: Why EMS Adverse Events Are Missed

options.A: Use the National EMS Information System (NEMSIS) demographic dataset alone because it standardizes all clinical benchmarking.
options.B: Use the Cardiac Arrest Registry to Enhance Survival (CARES) name only, without requiring specific arrest elements.
options.C: Use the Utstein template as the minimum standardized cardiac-arrest dataset.
options.D: Use local narrative review because cardiac arrest outcomes are too system-specific for standard fields.

EMS2026-3-1-4-1-04.rationale: The Utstein template provides a minimum standardized dataset for cardiac arrest performance benchmarking and outcome reporting. A and B confuse broader registry infrastructure with the needed arrest elements, and D prevents meaningful comparison across systems.
EMS2026-3-1-4-1-04.stem: An EMS agency wants to compare out-of-hospital cardiac arrest outcomes with neighboring systems. Current reports contain narrative summaries but inconsistent fields for witnessed status, bystander cardiopulmonary resuscitation, initial rhythm, shocks, return of circulation, survival, and neurologic status. Which data approach is best?
EMS2026-3-1-4-1-04.title: Utstein Cardiac Arrest Dataset
EMS2026-3-1-4-1-04.topic: Local, state, and national EMS data sources (including registries and databases)

options.C: Collect satisfaction comments from referred patients before changing implementation because uptake may improve with time.
options.D: Stratify process and outcome measures, identify barriers in the low-uptake group, and adapt implementation while tracking equity effects.

EMS2026-3-1-6-08.rationale: Implementation monitoring should stratify measures when equity is an aim because averages can hide persistent disparities. A masks the problem, B reinforces unequal uptake, and C delays practical improvement work.
EMS2026-3-1-6-08.stem: An EMS fall-prevention referral program reduces repeat 911 calls overall. Stratified data show referrals increased in high-income neighborhoods but barely changed in subsidized housing complexes. What should the implementation team do next?
EMS2026-3-1-6-08.title: Equity Monitoring During Implementation

EMS2026-3-2-1-1-07.citation: White LJ. Informed consent in EMS research. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:467.

options.A: A state EMS regulation requiring common medical oversight policies across agencies.
options.B: An institutional rule that investigators use when no federal human-subjects regulation applies.

options.D: The federal human-subjects protection policy at 45 Code of Federal Regulations Part 46.

EMS2026-3-2-1-1-07.rationale: The Common Rule is the federal policy for protection of human subjects, codified at 45 Code of Federal Regulations Part 46, Subpart A. A, B, and C misidentify it as a state, institutional, or drug-only rule.
EMS2026-3-2-1-1-07.stem: An EMS research lecture asks learners to identify the "Common Rule." Which description is most accurate?

EMS2026-3-2-1-1-07.topic: Ethical frameworks (e.g., Belmont Report, Common Rule, Final Rule)

options.B: Full board review is required because all EMS research involves emergency patients indirectly.
options.C: Exception from informed consent is required because respondents cannot sign consent while on duty.
options.D: No ethics review is possible because anonymous surveys fall outside human-subjects protections.

EMS2026-3-2-1-2-05.rationale: Exempt review may apply to less-than-minimal-risk activities that meet specific criteria, such as anonymous surveys or deidentified records. B overstates EMS research risk, C confuses clinician survey research with emergency patient research, and D incorrectly assumes anonymity removes review considerations.
EMS2026-3-2-1-2-05.stem: An EMS fellow proposes an anonymous survey of paramedics about continuing-education preferences. No patient data are collected, no identifiers are stored, and the topic is not sensitive. Which review category is most plausible?
EMS2026-3-2-1-2-05.title: Exempt Review in EMS Research
EMS2026-3-2-1-2-05.topic: Role of the Institutional Review Board

options.B: Exempt review because EMS research occurs before hospital registration and uses standard medications.
options.C: Medical-director approval only because treatment protocols are part of operational oversight.
options.D: Expedited review solely because the drugs are already used in emergency care.

EMS2026-3-2-1-2-07.rationale: More-than-minimal-risk prospective interventional research generally requires full IRB review. B and D understate risk, and C confuses clinical protocol authority with human-subjects review.
EMS2026-3-2-1-2-07.stem: An EMS investigator proposes randomizing hypotensive patients to two vasopressor strategies under exception from informed consent. The intervention changes emergency treatment and is more than minimal risk. Which institutional review board (IRB) pathway is generally required?

EMS2026-3-2-1-2-07.topic: Role of the Institutional Review Board

EMS2026-3-2-3-1-08.citation: Newgard CD, Lewis RJ. Statistical concepts for research in emergency medical services. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:504-505.

options.A: Interpret the alert as likely harmful because the interval includes a negative value.
options.B: Interpret the alert as definitely beneficial because the point estimate favors it.
options.C: Focus primarily on the p value because confidence intervals do not show clinical importance.
options.D: Imprecise interval including no benefit and meaningful benefit; interpret cautiously.

EMS2026-3-2-3-1-08.rationale: A confidence interval shows precision and the range of plausible effects; if it includes no effect, the result may not be statistically significant for a difference measure. A and B overstate one part of the interval, and C discards useful precision and clinical-importance information.
EMS2026-3-2-3-1-08.stem: A trial reports that a new prehospital sepsis alert shortened time to antibiotics by 8 minutes, with a 95 percent confidence interval from -2 to 18 minutes. Which interpretation is best?
EMS2026-3-2-3-1-08.title: Confidence Interval Interpretation

options.A: Sensitivity is 90 percent and specificity is 88 percent because false positives determine sensitivity.
options.B: Sensitivity is 88 percent and specificity is 15 percent because missed strokes determine both measures.
options.C: Sensitivity is 12 percent and specificity is 10 percent because those error rates are what quality teams should track.
options.D: Sensitivity 88 percent and specificity 90 percent using disease-present and disease-absent denominators.

EMS2026-3-2-3-2-07.rationale: Sensitivity is the probability of a positive test when disease is present, and specificity is the probability of a negative test when disease is absent. A swaps the denominators, B uses the false-positive count as specificity, and C reports error rates rather than sensitivity and specificity.
EMS2026-3-2-3-2-07.stem: A stroke-screen validation study reports that the screen is positive in 88 of 100 patients with stroke and negative in 135 of 150 patients without stroke. Which paired interpretation is correct?
EMS2026-3-2-3-2-07.title: Sensitivity And Specificity

options.A: The primary outcome is precise but may not be clinically or operationally meaningful enough for the study question.
options.B: The study is invalid because cardiac-arrest research cannot use time intervals as outcomes.
options.C: The study should use survival only because process measures have no role in EMS research.
options.D: A statistically significant 4-second difference is automatically important because cardiac arrest is time critical.

EMS2026-3-2-4-3-02.rationale: Outcome measures should matter clinically or operationally, not merely be precise. B and C overreject process measures, while D confuses statistical detectability with meaningful improvement.
EMS2026-3-2-4-3-02.stem: A proposed study of a new cardiac-arrest dashboard is powered to detect a 4-second reduction in time from monitor-on to first rhythm label. It will not measure compression fraction, shock interval, medication timing, survival, or neurologic outcome. Which critique is strongest?
EMS2026-3-2-4-3-02.title: Outcome Measure That Matters

options.A: Keep direct reporting to the incident commander and add a medical scribe to track unresolved resource and routing requests.
options.B: Convert each EMS group into a separate operations subsection so medical supervisors have narrower functional responsibility.
options.C: Place EMS functions under the transport group because patient movement is the common operational endpoint for field medical work.
options.D: Create an EMS branch to supervise related EMS groups and reduce the incident commander's direct span of control.

EMS2026-4-1-3-3-02.rationale: Branches are useful when groups or divisions exceed manageable span of control; an EMS branch can coordinate related EMS functions. A documents overload but does not relieve it, B uses the wrong organizational remedy, and C makes transport responsible for functions beyond its role.
EMS2026-4-1-3-3-02.stem: At a mass casualty incident (MCI), the incident commander is directly supervising triage, treatment, transport, ambulance staging, responder rehabilitation, medical supply, and family reunification groups. Radio traffic is congested and group supervisors cannot get timely decisions. Which Incident Command System (ICS) adjustment is best?
EMS2026-4-1-3-3-02.title: EMS Branch Span Of Control

options.A: Robert T. Stafford Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act.
options.B: Homeland Security Act.
options.C: Emergency Management Assistance Compact.
options.D: Federal Disaster Assistance Coordination Act.

EMS2026-4-1-4-1-03.rationale: The Stafford Act is the federal authority for presidential disaster declarations and federal disaster assistance. The Homeland Security Act created the Department of Homeland Security, the Emergency Management Assistance Compact supports interstate mutual aid, and the Federal Disaster Assistance Coordination Act is a plausible-sounding distractor rather than the controlling authority.
EMS2026-4-1-4-1-03.stem: A state emergency manager asks which authority established the modern process for requesting and obtaining a presidential disaster declaration and defines the type, scope, and conditions of federal disaster assistance. Which authority is best?
EMS2026-4-1-4-1-03.title: Stafford Act Disaster Declaration
EMS2026-4-1-4-1-03.topic: State and federal criteria for disaster declaration

options.A: EMAC recognition continues for the original deployment roster until the receiving state releases every assigned clinician.
options.B: EMAC recognition can transfer into routine employment if the agency documents competence and keeps the same medical director.
options.C: Temporary recognition ends with the emergency declaration, and usual affected-state licensing requirements apply.
options.D: Temporary recognition lasts until federal reimbursement closes, even if the state emergency declaration has ended.

EMS2026-4-1-4-2-05.rationale: Emergency Management Assistance Compact licensure recognition is tied to the emergency. Once the immediate emergency ends and the declaration is terminated, clinicians must comply with the affected state's normal licensing requirements. A, B, and D extend emergency authority beyond its intended boundary.
EMS2026-4-1-4-2-05.stem: A paramedic deployed under the Emergency Management Assistance Compact (EMAC) wants to continue working shifts in the affected state after the governor terminates the state of emergency. The local agency still needs help. What should the medical director explain?

options.A: Mutual aid mainly improves public messaging, staging assignments, and press coordination; spontaneous crews are otherwise equivalent once they arrive.
options.B: Self-deployed clinicians gain licensure and liability recognition after documenting patient contacts and receiving a mission number.
options.C: Official mutual aid is useful only for federally declared disasters; local and state events should rely on spontaneous availability.
options.D: Recognized mutual aid better addresses licensure, liability, worker protection, mission assignment, and resource accountability.

EMS2026-4-1-4-2-06.rationale: Disaster EMS mutual aid should be official and recognized because it creates clearer authority, resource tracking, licensure, liability, and worker-protection pathways. A and B minimize those protections, and C incorrectly limits mutual aid to federal declarations.
EMS2026-4-1-4-2-06.stem: After a neighboring state is struck by a tornado, a private ambulance service proposes sending crews immediately and arranging credentials later. The disaster coordinator instead recommends activation through recognized mutual-aid channels. Why is that distinction important?
EMS2026-4-1-4-2-06.title: Mutual Aid and Self-Deployment

EMS2026-4-1-7-3-3-01.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:301.
EMS2026-4-1-7-3-3-01.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Triage categories should remain fixed once assigned because changing them creates transport confusion.
options.B: Tags are unnecessary if patients are placed in geographic treatment areas.
options.C: Triage tags or marking systems should permit bidirectional category changes as patient condition changes.
options.D: Only immediate and dead categories need durable marking because other categories can wait for hospital reassessment.

EMS2026-4-1-7-3-3-01.rationale: Triage tags or marking systems should allow category changes in both directions as patient conditions change. A makes triage static, B treats an alternative marking method as eliminating category tracking, and D ignores delayed, minimal, and expectant patient flow.
EMS2026-4-1-7-3-3-01.stem: A mass-casualty plan uses adhesive triage tags that can be upgraded from delayed to immediate by adding a red sticker, but the design does not allow downgrading after reassessment or treatment. Which concern is most important?
EMS2026-4-1-7-3-3-01.title: Dynamic Triage Categories

EMS2026-4-1-8-1-3-01.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:331.
EMS2026-4-1-8-1-3-01.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Begin lifesaving care at the boundary, then move contaminated ambulatory patients directly to transport.
options.B: Move patients to the emergency department ambulance bay for faster decontamination and physician triage.
options.C: Prioritize visibly contaminated patients first and let asymptomatic patients wait near the clean tent.
options.D: Hold patients at the exposure boundary for decontamination before movement into clean areas.

EMS2026-4-1-8-1-3-01.rationale: Providers and patients should generally be decontaminated before exiting the exposure site to prevent contamination spread into clean areas. A and B move contamination into transport or hospital spaces, while C relies on visible contamination and symptoms too heavily.
EMS2026-4-1-8-1-3-01.stem: After a chemical release, several ambulatory patients begin walking from the exposure area toward ambulances and a clean treatment tent. Formal decontamination lanes are being set up. Which instruction is best?
EMS2026-4-1-8-1-3-01.title: Decontamination Before Leaving the Exposure Site

EMS2026-4-1-8-4-04.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2021:366.
EMS2026-4-1-8-4-04.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Wait for decontamination because radioactive contamination is usually the immediate life threat after dispersal.
options.B: Proceed with lifesaving care using contamination precautions because bleeding is the immediate life threat.
options.C: Defer bleeding control to the hospital radiation safety officer because field care spreads contamination.
options.D: Skip decontamination once the patient leaves the hot zone because contamination no longer affects EMS operations.

EMS2026-4-1-8-4-04.rationale: In radiological incidents, contamination is usually not the immediate life threat, so lifesaving care should not be withheld while decontamination is completed. A and C invert priorities, while D erases the need for contamination control.
EMS2026-4-1-8-4-04.stem: After a radiological dispersal device incident, a patient has severe extremity bleeding and possible external contamination. A responder asks whether hemorrhage control should wait until full decontamination is completed. Which answer is best?
EMS2026-4-1-8-4-04.title: Radiological Contamination and Lifesaving Care

EMS2026-4-2-1-5-01.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:264.
EMS2026-4-2-1-5-01.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Historical patient volume is sufficient if the onsite medical team can request ambulances after routine demand rises.
options.B: Mass-gathering plans should avoid outside resource requests until onsite treatment areas are fully saturated.
options.C: Escalation tools are needed because routine event demand can saturate resources before a mass-casualty incident.
options.D: Multi-patient incidents at mass gatherings should be managed separately from the event medical plan.

EMS2026-4-2-1-5-01.rationale: A mass-casualty incident during a mass gathering is dangerous because emergency services may already be saturated by routine event demand, and historical planning may not be enough. A and B wait too long, while D separates escalation from the plan that must absorb it.
EMS2026-4-2-1-5-01.stem: A stadium concert medical plan is based on prior years' routine patient volume. During the event, heat illness and intoxication already occupy most onsite teams when a partial bleacher collapse creates multiple trauma patients. What planning lesson is most important?
EMS2026-4-2-1-5-01.title: Mass Gathering Escalation Risk

EMS2026-4-3-1-07.citation: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons; 2015:366.
EMS2026-4-3-1-07.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Start rehabilitation only when ambient heat index is high or a responder reports dizziness, chest pain, or confusion.
options.B: Start rehabilitation when protective-clothing work reaches 20 minutes or concerning signs are identified.
options.C: Start rehabilitation after one hour of total scene time, unless command declares a high-humidity incident.
options.D: Start rehabilitation after transport decisions are complete so medical staffing is not pulled from patient triage.

EMS2026-4-3-1-07.rationale: Emergency incident rehabilitation should begin when work in fire suppression or hazardous-material protective clothing lasts 20 minutes or more, and symptoms should also trigger withdrawal and monitoring. A omits the time threshold, C sets it too late, and D treats responder rehabilitation as secondary to triage rather than part of scene safety.
EMS2026-4-3-1-07.stem: A fire department is revising rehabilitation procedures for EMS support at structure fires and hazardous-material scenes. The draft says rehabilitation begins only after a responder requests it or after a second air cylinder is used. Which threshold should be added?
EMS2026-4-3-1-07.title: Emergency Incident Rehabilitation Trigger

EMS2026-4-4-6-1-02.citation: Schwartz RB, McManus JG Jr, Croushorn J, et al. Tactical Medicine-Competency-Based Guidelines. Prehosp Emerg Care. 2011;15(1):78.
EMS2026-4-4-6-1-02.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Hospital destination planning, because once definitive care is identified the tactical medical plan is complete.
options.B: Medical threat assessment across infiltration, actions at the objective, and exfiltration.
options.C: Rehabilitation planning, because tactical medical risk is primarily responder fatigue.
options.D: Mass-gathering medical planning, because tactical and public-event risk models are interchangeable.

EMS2026-4-4-6-1-02.rationale: A tactical medical plan should consider medical threats during infiltration, actions at the objective, and exfiltration. A is too narrow, C captures only one risk, and D applies the wrong planning model.
EMS2026-4-4-6-1-02.stem: A tactical plan lists the nearest trauma center and medevac landing zone but does not analyze the long wooded approach, breaching hazards, confined-space entry, or evacuation under fire. Which medical planning element is missing?
EMS2026-4-4-6-1-02.title: Tactical Medical Threat Across Mission Phases

EMS2026-4-4-6-1-05.citation: Schwartz RB, McManus JG Jr, Croushorn J, et al. Tactical Medicine-Competency-Based Guidelines. Prehosp Emerg Care. 2011;15(1):78.
EMS2026-4-4-6-1-05.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Review supplies after the tactical plan is complete, then recommend extra hemorrhage control, airway, and evacuation equipment for contingencies.
options.B: Participate in medical threat assessment so hazards, evacuation, casualty collection, and care capabilities are built into the operation.
options.C: Restrict input to clinician credentialing, communications planning, and postincident review because tactical commanders determine scene medical risk.
options.D: Attend only the postoperation review because pre-event medical planning can blur tactical command and medical oversight roles.

EMS2026-4-4-6-1-05.rationale: Tactical medical planning includes medical threat assessment, and the medical director has an oversight role before the operation. A and D make medical oversight reactive, and C separates credentialing from the actual medical-risk plan.
EMS2026-4-4-6-1-05.stem: A tactical team plans a high-risk warrant service at a rural property with limited egress, dogs, possible chemicals, and long transport time. The medical director is invited only to review the trauma bag after the plan is finalized. Which role is more appropriate?
EMS2026-4-4-6-1-05.title: Tactical Medical Threat Assessment

EMS2026-4-5-1-1-04.citation: Hawkins SC, Millin MG, Smith WR. Wilderness EMS. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. Wiley; 2021:401.
EMS2026-4-5-1-1-04.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Add trained use of Stokes baskets, ropes, and rigging with the rescue system.
options.B: Extra ambulance cots, stair chairs, and hospital transfer sheets staged at the trailhead.
options.C: Improvised rope systems assembled by medical crews when technical teams are delayed.
options.D: Lightweight scoop stretchers only, because medical packaging should stay separate from rope operations.

EMS2026-4-5-1-1-04.rationale: Wilderness and technical rescue equipment may include Stokes baskets, rope, and rigging for high-angle, cave, water, and related rescue environments. B is conventional transport equipment, C is unsafe without training, and D separates patient packaging from the technical rescue system.
EMS2026-4-5-1-1-04.stem: A medical director is reviewing equipment for an EMS team that supports high-angle and cave rescues. The current cache is built around standard ambulance stretchers and soft goods. Which addition best matches wilderness and technical rescue needs?
EMS2026-4-5-1-1-04.title: High-Angle Rescue Equipment Planning

EMS2026-4-5-1-2-03.citation: Cone DC. Technical rescue, confined space, and limited access situations. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. Wiley; 2021:389.
EMS2026-4-5-1-2-03.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Test temperature, noise, and communication before entry, then rely on rescuers' respirators for atmospheric protection.
options.B: Number of rescuers and rope length, because atmospheric hazards are addressed only after patient contact.
options.C: Patient medication history and downtime, because entry safety is determined by medical urgency.
options.D: Oxygen content, flammability, toxicity, and explosive hazards through atmospheric testing before and during entry.

EMS2026-4-5-1-2-03.rationale: Confined-space entry requires atmospheric testing and monitoring for oxygen, flammability, toxicity, and explosive hazards before and during entry. A addresses possible operational issues but not the required hazard set, B delays testing, and C lets urgency override entry safety.
EMS2026-4-5-1-2-03.stem: A worker is unconscious inside a storage tank. Fire-rescue entry technicians are preparing retrieval, and EMS is asked what medical-rescue planning issue must be verified before and during entry. Which answer best fits confined-space precautions?
EMS2026-4-5-1-2-03.title: Confined-Space Atmospheric Testing

EMS2026-4-5-1-5-04.citation: Cone DC. Technical rescue, confined space, and limited access situations. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. Wiley; 2021:389.
EMS2026-4-5-1-5-04.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Direct the technical method because medical limb-salvage priorities supersede rescue-team judgment.
options.B: Decline to advise until the arm is free because movement mechanics are a technical-rescue domain.
options.C: Explain likely anatomic injury, perfusion risk, analgesia needs, and movement consequences while rescue specialists choose the technical method.
options.D: Focus on transport destination because extrication mechanics will not change immediate EMS care.

EMS2026-4-5-1-5-04.rationale: Medical personnel should contribute anatomy and physiology information without taking over technical rescue. A oversteps, B withholds useful expertise, and D misses a moment where medical input can affect injury during movement.
EMS2026-4-5-1-5-04.stem: A worker's forearm is trapped in rollers. Reversing the rollers may worsen degloving but is fastest; cutting the frame is slower but may reduce tissue shear. The rescue officer asks EMS for input before choosing. What is the best medical role?
EMS2026-4-5-1-5-04.title: Medical Advice During Machinery Disentanglement

EMS2026-4-5-5-01.citation: Cone DC. Technical rescue, confined space, and limited access situations. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. Wiley; 2021:388.
EMS2026-4-5-5-01.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Stage analgesia, monitoring, airway, warming, and crush-injury supplies near the rescue group before patient access is achieved.
options.B: Wait for direct patient contact before moving medications forward because rescue estimates often change during disentanglement.
options.C: Stage only a stretcher and splints because medical treatment should begin after the technical rescue plan is complete.
options.D: Keep advanced equipment at the ambulance to preserve medication accountability and avoid clutter around the rescue site.

EMS2026-4-5-5-01.rationale: Operational medical planning should anticipate likely needs before direct access. A makes time-sensitive tools available when access occurs. B, C, and D describe reasonable logistical concerns but create a predictable treatment delay.
EMS2026-4-5-5-01.stem: A trench-rescue patient is visible but not reachable for an estimated 25 minutes. The rescue group predicts crush injury, pain, hypothermia risk, and possible airway difficulty once access is made. Medical equipment remains in the ambulance 200 yards away. Which pre-access action is best?
EMS2026-4-5-5-01.title: Equipment Staging Before Access

EMS2026-4-6-2-3-03.citation: Hawkins SC, Millin MG, Smith WR. Wilderness EMS. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. Wiley; 2021:399.
EMS2026-4-6-2-3-03.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Splint in the position found, provide analgesia, and request a technical carryout because reduction risk may exceed benefit.
options.B: Begin descent with manual support because transport risk is lower than the risk of field reduction.
options.C: Sedate deeply for reduction because wilderness dislocations require analgesia before any extrication.
options.D: Attempt protocolized reduction if trained and authorized to reduce evacuation risk.

EMS2026-4-6-2-3-03.rationale: In wilderness EMS, trained clinicians may reduce selected shoulder dislocations to allow self-evacuation and lower patient and rescuer risk. A ignores the evacuation hazard, B undertreats a barrier to movement, and C assumes deep sedation rather than matching analgesia and reduction to protocol and capability.
EMS2026-4-6-2-3-03.stem: A trained wilderness EMS clinician reaches a climber with an isolated anterior shoulder dislocation on a ledge two miles from the trailhead. Distal pulse and sensation are intact, pain prevents self-evacuation, weather is worsening, and litter carry would expose rescuers to a steep traverse. Which field decision is most defensible?
EMS2026-4-6-2-3-03.title: Wilderness Shoulder Reduction
EMS2026-4-6-2-3-03.topic: Field reduction of dislocations, subluxations, or fractures

EMS2026-4-6-2-4-02.citation: Busko J. Cold exposure illness and injury. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. Wiley; 2021:396-397.
EMS2026-4-6-2-4-02.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Keep the patient horizontal, handle gently, prevent heat loss, begin appropriate rewarming, and arrange careful evacuation.
options.B: Walk the patient with assistance, use exertion to generate heat, protect from wind, and reassess mentation after reaching the trailhead.
options.C: Sit the patient upright, provide warm sweet drinks if tolerated, insulate the torso, and package after shivering stops or mental status worsens.
options.D: Massage cold extremities, encourage limb movement, add chemical warmers to hands and feet, and delay packaging until peripheral circulation improves.

EMS2026-4-6-2-4-02.rationale: Moderate to severe hypothermia calls for horizontal positioning, gentle handling, prevention of exertion, and rewarming during careful evacuation. B, C, and D risk exertion, instability, afterdrop, or tissue injury.
EMS2026-4-6-2-4-02.stem: A wilderness EMS team reaches a shivering, confused hiker found supine in snow after overnight exposure. The patient has a weak radial pulse and is unable to stand. The trail is narrow, and bystanders want to walk the patient to a warmer trailhead. Which handling plan is best?
EMS2026-4-6-2-4-02.title: Hypothermia Handling Priority

EMS2026-4-6-2-4-04.citation: Busko J. Cold exposure illness and injury. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. Wiley; 2021:399.
EMS2026-4-6-2-4-04.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Begin warm-water thawing immediately because earlier reperfusion generally improves tissue outcome.
options.B: Massage the toes during evacuation to restore circulation while preventing refreezing and monitoring distal sensation.
options.C: Use dry heat from a stove until the toes soften, then allow the patient to walk out with insulated boots.
options.D: Protect frozen tissue and defer thawing until refreezing can be prevented and controlled rewarming can be completed.

EMS2026-4-6-2-4-04.rationale: Frostbite should be thawed in the field only when refreezing can be prevented and controlled warm-water rewarming can be completed. A risks refreezing injury, B adds mechanical tissue damage, and C uses uncontrolled heat with later trauma exposure.
EMS2026-4-6-2-4-04.stem: A backcountry team reaches a climber with hard, pale toes after a cold overnight bivouac. The team has warm water capability, but evacuation will require several hours of walking through freezing conditions after treatment. Which field plan is best?
EMS2026-4-6-2-4-04.title: Field Thawing of Frostbite

EMS2026-4-7-3-05.citation: Ulintz AJ, O'Connor LC, Heffern TM, et al. Mobile Integrated Health Care and Community Paramedicine: A Position Statement and Resource Document of NAEMSP. Prehosp Emerg Care. 2025:3.
EMS2026-4-7-3-05.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: A monthly patient-satisfaction survey mailed after selected nontransports to monitor experience.
options.B: A staffing model that assigns the same clinicians to low-acuity calls and relies on their disposition judgment.
options.C: Use a community-education campaign to reduce low-acuity 911 demand before dispatch occurs.
options.D: Point-of-care testing incorporated into protocols that direct patients to appropriate levels of care.

EMS2026-4-7-3-05.rationale: System-navigation mobile integrated health models may use point-of-care testing to help direct patients to appropriate care levels. A is retrospective, B affects staffing rather than clinical stratification, and C may affect demand but not individual disposition.
EMS2026-4-7-3-05.stem: A mobile integrated health program is designed to redirect selected low-acuity 911 callers to clinics, home visits, or emergency department transport. The medical director wants a tool that helps determine level of care during system navigation rather than merely after arrival. Which resource best fits that role?
EMS2026-4-7-3-05.title: Point-Of-Care Testing In Mobile Integrated Health
EMS2026-4-7-3-05.topic: Resources (e.g., equipment, medications, or point-of-care testing)

EMS2026-4-7-4-05.citation: Kroll M, Munjal KG. Mobile integrated health and community paramedicine. In: Cone DC, Brice JH, Delbridge TR, Myers JB, eds. Emergency Medical Services: Clinical Practice and Systems Oversight. 3rd ed. Wiley; 2021:410.
EMS2026-4-7-4-05.intake_notes: Backfilled from existing website question bank when lifecycle tracking was standardized.

options.A: Contract approval, ambulance deployment, billing compliance, referral marketing, and payer negotiations for enrolled patients.
options.B: Patient satisfaction review, clinic scheduling, equipment purchasing, transport billing, and social-media communication.
options.C: Discipline decisions, dispatch staffing, home-health licensure, hospital bed management, and paramedic hiring.
options.D: Needs assessment, care goals, stakeholder coordination, regulatory compliance, safety, documentation, and education.

EMS2026-4-7-4-05.rationale: Mobile integrated health medical direction includes needs assessment, patient-care goals, stakeholder coordination, regulatory compliance, safety and quality, documentation and reporting, and education. A, B, and C contain operational or administrative pieces but omit several core medical-oversight duties.
EMS2026-4-7-4-05.stem: A health system is building a mobile integrated health (MIH) program. The draft assigns the medical director to approve protocols once and review charts quarterly. Which added responsibilities best complete the oversight role?
EMS2026-4-7-4-05.title: Mobile Integrated Health Medical Direction
